5 min read
Failing to consider second- and third-order consequences is the cause of
a lot of painfully bad decisions, and it is especially deadly when the �rst
inferior option con�rms your own biases. Never seize on the �rst
available option, no matter how good it seems, before you’ve asked
questions and explored. — Ray Dalio
Many marketing truths are counter-intuitive because they require
second-order thinking. Seeing those truths requires a shift in
perspective and the con�dence to look beyond the obvious answer
in plain sight.
It’s not easy, but it’s important. And when you make second-order
thinking a habit, you will have a powerful competitive advantage.
Permission Before Promotion is one example of a powerful
counter-intuitive decision.
When someone joins your email list, it’s easy to assume that they
have given you open-ended permission to email them. After all,
they can one-click unsubscribe at any point, right?
Strategic Upstream Decisions:
Permission Before Promotion
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 8 --

We believe there’s a better alternative. We’ll describe why,
conceptually, in this module, and then explain how this works in
practice in the Bridge Email module.
Let’s start with an organizing question: Do you believe that 100% of
your subscribers — both prospects and customers — will be
interested in purchasing every o�er — a�liate and/or your own —
you send them?
What if we stack the deck in your favor and ask only about
customers — people who have already demonstrated a
willingness to spend money with you. Do you believe that 100% of
your customers will be interested in purchasing every offer you
send them?
No. Not even close.
An email list of 1,000 True Fans will never produce 1,000 sales, or
even 500 sales, and very likely not even 100 sales at one time.
Yet, it’s common behavior to send promotions to everyone. Or, if
not to an entire email list, everyone within a segment (prospects vs.
customers, customers of Blue Widgets for your Red Widget
promotion, etc.).
The �rst reality to accept is that, at any point in time, most of your
subscribers (even customers) will not be interested in purchasing
your product (or af�liate offer), enrolling in your course, or paying
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 8 --

attention to your promotion.
Some percentage will be interested. And some percentage of those
who are interested will buy. But it won’t be everyone, and it won’t
be most people.
So what’s the solution then?
Instead of sending offers to everyone all the time, we ask for
explicit permission before sending promotions. We do this with
Bridge Emails.
Bridge Emails ask for permission to promote. Everyone who raises
their hand expects, anticipates, and WANTS to see the promotion
that follows.
That active participation from the audience has an exceptionally
powerful framing effect.
When we emphasize relationships over transactions, we
acknowledge that trust and attention are scarce resources.
Trust is incredibly fragile, and attention doesn’t scale (we can’t do
more than one thing at a time, and everyone is competing for the
slice of time in which that ‘one thing’ happens).
When we keep sending offers to someone not interested at that
speci�c moment in time, we whittle away our trust equity, and
when we lose someone’s trust and attention, it’s often lost for
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 8 --

good.
This is why we choose to be so protective about what we send to
our subscribers — prospects and customers; everyone. Being
relevant is a superpower, particularly when you’re in this for the
long game.
The Bridge Email is a transition engineered deliberately to
identify interested people and to present an opportunity for
them to self-select by raising their hands.
When we introduce this subtle friction, we’re demonstrating
respect for our subscribers’ time and attention. We’re also
intentionally creating a barrier that increases tension.
This creates a �rewall between our interested-now prospects
and everyone else. (This also creates a signal that can help predict
and analyze performance.)
It’s human nature to want something that’s either hard to get or
not available. The Bridge Email moves people from passive
observers to actively engaged participants.
This image represents your email list of subscribers (or a segment
within the whole list).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 8 --

The three ?/?/? counters above each subscriber represent the
invisible, largely subconscious, dynamic forces motivating
someone’s behavior and level of attention at any point in time.
Conceptually, these three numbers represent a prospect’s present-
moment intensity of his or her ‘jobs to be done,’ ‘pains,’ and ‘gains.’
▪ Jobs (important to insigni�cant: 10-0)
▪ Pains (extreme to moderate: 10-0)
▪ Gains (absolutely essential to “nice to have”: 10-0)
(See Audience & Offer Masterclass, Part II for a deep dive on this.)
Taken together, these three factors represent the level of interest
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 8 --

and attention a prospect brings to your offer right now.
At any moment in time, a job to be done can be urgent and painful
enough that the timing of your promotion is a perfect �t, or
meh and not urgent at all beyond some initial curiosity.
Our �rst priority for any promotion (evergreen or time-bound), is
to identify the people who are most likely to have these three
counters as high as possible and pull them in, focusing their
attention for a promotion, and creating anticipation for the
results and experience your offer will make possible for them.
In a perfect world, we’re looking to identify and call out the
10/10/10 people and have them express their intent, while also
�ltering out the 0/0/0 people.
But, because life is anything but perfect, conceptually, we are
looking to attract 7, 8, 9, and 10 combos. Maybe even some 6s. We
want to actively �lter out (dissuade) everyone else. Everyone!.
9s and 10s are the aces in our deck. There are fewer of them, so we
want to attract them all because they likely represent:
▪ Superfans,
▪ Early Adopters,
▪ People experiencing the most dissonance,
▪ People experiencing the most pain,
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 8 --

▪ People experiencing the strongest pull and desire for change
and will do almost anything to solve their problem (or
pursue a need or desire).
This is why context and framing in the Bridge Email is critical for
the success of your promotions. You want a high concentration of
7s and above to self-identify and experience a level of excitement
and anticipation for what is about to unfold.
In our experience, this is the most effective framework to identify
the essential needs of an audience and match those needs
precisely to the product or service you offer.
This is the exact same exercise we have taught our $20K/mo
consulting clients. This exercise will inform and color the
downstream emails you write for your promotion and reveal many
ways to frame your offer.
People buy things for many reasons (functional, emotional, and
social). Understanding the full spectrum of reasons someone
might buy what you have to offer is powerful beyond the obvious
functional bene�ts.
The Audience & Offer Masterclass (AOM) teaches this exact
framework, and we’ve made it available to all our customers for
free. AOM is a good place to start before you create your �rst
Bridge Email.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 8 --

When you accept permission before promotions as your new
reality, and commit to it with your behavior, every promotion —
every single one — will become signi�cantly easier, more
enjoyable, and much more effective.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com
